skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: Guides AI coding agents through root cause investigation before proposing bug fixes.
skill.summary: Debug bugs and test failures with a structured root cause process.
skill.author: obra

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, environment, and sensitive-access patterns, but most are markdown examples or diagrams rather than executed skill behavior. The real risks are an included shell helper that runs local tests from user-supplied inputs, diagnostic examples that may expose local environment details, and directive test prompts that could influence an AI if loaded out of context.

medium_findings.title: Local Test Runner Script Uses User-Supplied Paths
medium_findings.description: find-polluter.sh accepts a pollution path and test pattern, expands matching files, and runs npm test for each match. This is legitimate debugging tooling, but it can execute project test code and inspect filesystem paths chosen by the user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly executes npm test over files selected from user-supplied inputs. Risk is bounded to local project execution and no exfiltration behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Directive Prompts Included In Test Fixtures
medium_findings.description: The pressure test files contain instructions such as "You must choose and act" and "Which do you choose?". They appear to be legitimate test scenarios, but marketplace packaging should treat them as untrusted prompt content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is clearly directive to an AI agent, but it is framed as a test fixture rather than an attempt to override audit rules or claim authority.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Diagnostic Commands In Examples
low_findings.description: The skill shows examples that print environment propagation and macOS signing identity state. These examples do not exfiltrate data, but copied output could expose local configuration or signing metadata in logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in documentation and can reveal environment or keychain state. They are diagnostic examples with no network sink or credential collection flow.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto And Backtick Matches Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Flagged high-severity weak-crypto and Ruby backtick patterns occur in markdown fences, Graphviz snippets, timestamps, timeout examples, or explanatory text. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation or Ruby shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirmed these locations are diagrams or markdown examples. They do not contain executable weak cryptography or Ruby backtick execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Executes Project Test Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper script invokes npm test while searching for state pollution. Users should review test patterns before running it in repositories with untrusted tests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is direct and intentional. The risk depends on the trust level of the local repository tests.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment And Signing-State Diagnostics
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include env inspection and macOS signing identity commands. These are useful for debugging but can reveal local state if pasted into shared logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documentation examples with legitimate debugging purpose. They still expose sensitive local metadata when copied verbatim.

content.user_title: Debug Bugs With Root Cause Analysis
content.value_statement: Random fixes can hide the real cause and create new failures. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through evidence-based debugging before implementation.
actual_capabilities: Enforces root cause investigation before proposing fixes.
Provides a four-phase process for reproducing, tracing, testing, and verifying bugs.
Guides comparison against working examples and reference implementations.
Includes techniques for tracing bad data through call chains.
Shows condition-based waiting patterns for flaky asynchronous tests.
Includes a local helper script for finding tests that create unwanted state.
limitations: It does not diagnose bugs automatically without project evidence.
It may slow urgent changes when emergency mitigation is required first.
The helper script assumes npm test is the correct test command.
Some examples need adaptation for non-TypeScript or non-Node projects.

use_cases.title: Investigate Test Failures
use_cases.description: Use the process to reproduce the failure, trace the bad value, and create a targeted regression test.

use_cases.title: Stabilize Flaky CI Runs
use_cases.description: Replace arbitrary sleeps with condition-based waiting and verify the actual state that the test needs.

use_cases.title: Improve Incident Debugging
use_cases.description: Gather evidence across service boundaries before changing production code or adding broad workarounds.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Bug Investigation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a new failure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic debugging for this failure. Read the error, identify reproduction steps, and list the evidence needed before any fix.

prompt_templates.title: Trace A Bad Value
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for deep stack issues.
prompt_templates.prompt: Trace this incorrect value backward through the call chain. Show each caller, the value passed, and the first source of corruption.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze A Flaky Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this flaky test for arbitrary waiting. Propose condition-based checks and explain what each wait should verify.

prompt_templates.title: Review Failed Fix Attempts
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt after several failed attempts.
prompt_templates.prompt: We tried multiple fixes and the bug remains. Stop implementation, reassess the architecture, and identify the assumptions each failed fix disproved.

output_examples.input: A payment status test sometimes receives pending instead of completed.
output: First reproduce the failure and confirm whether timing is the only variable.
Trace where the status changes from pending to completed.
Replace sleep calls with a wait for the completion event or persisted state.
Add a regression test that fails before the root cause fix.

output_examples.input: A build signs locally but fails in CI.
output: Check each boundary: workflow secrets, build script environment, signing tool state, and final command arguments.
Identify the first layer where the expected identity disappears.
Fix propagation at that layer and avoid changing the signing command blindly.

output_examples.input: Several attempted fixes created new symptoms in different modules.
output: Stop adding patches and document the failed hypotheses.
Look for shared state, hidden coupling, or an invalid architecture assumption.
Discuss the design before attempting another code change.

best_practices: Collect reproduction steps and current evidence before writing a fix.
Change one variable at a time so verification stays meaningful.
Create a failing test before implementing the confirmed root cause fix.
anti_patterns: Adding retries, sleeps, or guards before understanding why the failure occurs.
Bundling refactors with bug fixes during the investigation phase.
Continuing with a fourth patch after several failed hypotheses.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it for bugs, failed tests, build failures, performance issues, and unexpected behavior before proposing a fix.

faq.question: Does it replace debugging tools?
faq.answer: No. It tells the AI how to use evidence from logs, tests, stack traces, and code review.

faq.question: Can it help during production incidents?
faq.answer: Yes, but emergency mitigation may come first. Use the process to avoid permanent fixes based on guesses.

faq.question: Why does it require a failing test?
faq.answer: A failing test proves the bug exists and confirms that the final change fixes the right behavior.

faq.question: Is the included shell script required?
faq.answer: No. It is optional tooling for finding tests that create unwanted local state.

faq.question: Does it work outside TypeScript projects?
faq.answer: Yes. The process is language independent, but several examples use TypeScript and npm.
